title: Where AI Struggles
intro: You're building a small e-commerce app and you've been using AI for everything. Things have been going great -- until they aren't. A customer reports they were charged twice for the same order.

teach.title: AI Code Can Be Like a Beautiful Bridge With Weak Foundations
teach.body: AI-generated code often looks clean and professional on the surface. But underneath, it might have subtle problems -- like a bridge that looks great but has a crack in a support beam. The code compiles, the tests pass, but there's a logic error waiting to cause real damage.

teach.title: AI Is Like a Spell Checker That Can't Read
teach.body: A spell checker catches typos perfectly but has no idea if your sentence actually makes sense. It can fix 'teh' to 'the' but won't notice if you said 'the car flew to the grocery store.' AI-generated code is similar -- it looks syntactically correct and follows conventions, but may contain logic that makes no real-world sense.

teach.title: Common AI Failure Modes
teach.body:
AI-generated code commonly fails in these ways:

1. **Duplicate logic** -- AI loses track during editing and repeats operations
2. **Missing edge cases** -- it handles the happy path but not errors or empty inputs
3. **Outdated patterns** -- it uses APIs or syntax from old library versions
4. **Confident but wrong** -- the code looks correct but has subtle logic bugs

teach.title: The Critical Code Rule
teach.body: Any code that handles **money, security, or user data** needs extra scrutiny. AI is great for scaffolding and UI, but payment processing, authentication, and data handling should always be reviewed line by line by a human. Add tests as safety nets for critical paths.
Knowing AI's weak spots doesn't slow you down -- it speeds you up. You'll catch bugs before your users do, which means fewer refunds, better reviews, and more trust in the products you ship.
AI code can look perfect and still have subtle bugs. Always review critical logic yourself, especially anything involving money, security, or user data. Write tests for important code paths.

start.narrative: A customer emails that they were charged $49.99 twice for a single order. You check the database and confirm the duplicate charge. The checkout code was written entirely by AI. What's your first step?

choices.text: Read through the AI-generated checkout code yourself
Ask AI to fix the double-charge bug
Refund the customer and hope it doesn't happen again
Remove the duplicate chargeCard call and add a safeguard to prevent double charges
Ask AI to rewrite the whole checkout flow

ask-ai-fix.narrative: You paste the code into AI and ask it to fix the double charge. The AI says 'I don't see any issues with this code, it looks correct.' AI can't always spot its own mistakes, especially when the code is syntactically valid. The duplicate call is logically wrong but structurally fine.

choices.text: Read through the code yourself line by line

just-refund.narrative: You refund the customer, but the next day three more customers report the same issue. The bug is still there. Ignoring the root cause just delays the inevitable -- and costs your business money and trust.

choices.text: Okay, time to actually look at the code

good-fix.narrative: You remove the duplicate chargeCard call and add an idempotency key to prevent double charges even if the function is accidentally called twice. You also add a test that verifies chargeCard is only called once per order. How do you prevent this in the future?

choices.text: Always review AI-generated code for payment and security logic, and write tests for critical paths
Stop using AI for the checkout flow entirely

partial-fix.narrative: Removing the duplicate line fixes the immediate bug, but without a safeguard, a future code change (or another AI edit) could reintroduce the problem. Production payment code needs defensive programming.

choices.text: Add an idempotency check and a test

ai-rewrite.narrative: The AI rewrites the checkout flow. It looks different, but you have no way to know if it has other subtle bugs without reading it carefully. Replacing AI code with more unreviewed AI code doesn't solve the underlying problem.

choices.text: Actually read through the code this time

end-best.narrative: You've fixed the bug, added a safeguard, written a test, and established a rule: all payment-related code gets human review. AI is still useful for the checkout UI and non-critical logic, but the money-handling code gets extra scrutiny.
end-best.feedback: This is the right approach. AI is great at many things, but financial logic, security, and critical business rules need human oversight. The key lesson: use AI to speed up development, but always review the critical paths yourself and add tests as safety nets.

end-okay.narrative: Avoiding AI entirely for checkout works, but it's overly cautious. AI can still help with the UI, validation, and non-payment parts of the flow. The real lesson is to review critical code, not to avoid AI altogether.
end-okay.feedback: You fixed the bug, but the takeaway is too extreme. You don't need to ban AI from your checkout flow -- you just need to review the critical parts (payment, auth, data handling) more carefully. AI can still help with boilerplate, UI, and non-critical logic.
